attributes.stem:
A 62-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of a painless lesion on his right foot. He has tried various topical treatments without improvement. He recalls having a plantar wart many years ago but notes this lesion is more persistent. His medical history includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes diagnosed eight years ago. He takes metformin, gliclazide, and perindopril. He has a 20 pack-year smoking history and drinks two cans of beer daily.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is symmetrical loss of hair on both shins with associated dry, flaky skin. Femoral and popliteal pulses are good bilaterally. The dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are palpable in both feet but weaker on the right compared to the left. There is reduced protective sensation in both feet with diminished proprioception, light touch, and vibration sense. His ankle-brachial index is 0.9 on the left leg and 0.8 on the right. His HbA1c is 8.5% (<6.5). A right foot X-ray shows no evidence of osteomyelitis. The lesion appears as a shallow ulcer on the plantar aspect of his right foot adjacent to the 3rd/4th metatarsal heads (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Referral to podiatry and multi-disciplinary diabetic foot clinic
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a classic diabetic neuropathic ulcer located at a pressure point on the plantar aspect of the foot. The clinical features supporting this diagnosis include the painless nature of the lesion (due to peripheral neuropathy), poorly controlled diabetes (HbA1c 8.5%), and evidence of peripheral neuropathy on examination. The ulcer's location adjacent to the metatarsal heads is typical, as these areas experience high pressure during walking, especially when foot biomechanics are altered by diabetic neuropathy.

Referral to podiatry and multi-disciplinary diabetic foot clinic is the most appropriate next step in management. This approach addresses the multiple factors contributing to diabetic foot ulcers and provides comprehensive care. The podiatrist will perform wound debridement, recommend appropriate offloading strategies (such as total contact casting or removable cast walkers), and provide education on foot care. The multidisciplinary team will address glycaemic control, vascular status, infection management, and other contributing factors. Evidence shows that multidisciplinary care significantly reduces amputation rates and improves healing outcomes.

Swab and culture may be appropriate if there are clinical signs of infection, such as erythema, warmth, purulence, or increasing pain. However, this ulcer appears clean and shallow without obvious signs of infection. While microbiology can guide antibiotic therapy when needed, it is not the priority in this non-infected ulcer where the fundamental issue is pressure and poor healing due to neuropathy and possibly mild ischaemia.

Topical silver sulfadiazine dressing is not indicated as first-line treatment for non-infected diabetic foot ulcers. While various wound dressings may be used as part of comprehensive care, dressings alone without addressing the underlying pressure and biomechanical issues will not lead to ulcer healing. Additionally, silver dressings are generally reserved for infected wounds or those at high risk of infection.

Oral antibiotics are not indicated in the absence of clinical signs of infection. Unnecessary antibiotic use contributes to antimicrobial resistance and may cause adverse effects. Current guidelines recommend against prophylactic antibiotics for non-infected diabetic foot ulcers.

Custom orthotic footwear alone, while an important component of management, is insufficient as a sole intervention. Effective management requires a comprehensive approach including wound care, offloading, infection control if present, vascular assessment, and metabolic control. Custom footwear is more appropriate for prevention of ulcer recurrence after healing or for preventing first ulcers in high-risk feet.
learningPoints: Diabetic neuropathic ulcers commonly occur at pressure points such as metatarsal heads due to altered foot biomechanics and loss of protective sensation.
Management of diabetic foot ulcers requires a multidisciplinary approach including podiatry, vascular assessment, and optimisation of glycaemic control.
Offloading pressure from the ulcer site is a critical component of diabetic foot ulcer management.
references: Schaper, Nicolaas C., et al. "Practical guidelines on the prevention and management of diabetic foot disease (IWGDF 2019 update)." Diabetes/Metabolism Research and Reviews 36 (2020): e3266. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32176447/
Reardon, R. et al (2020). RACGP - Diabetic foot ulcer. Australian Journal of General Practice. doi: 10.31128/AJGP-11-19-5161 Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32416652/

Armstrong DG, Tan TW, Boulton AJM, Bus SA. Diabetic Foot Ulcers: A Review. JAMA. 2023 Jul 3;330(1):62-75. doi: 10.1001/jama.2023.10578. PMID: 37395769; PMCID: PMC10723802. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37395769/

Everett E, Mathioudakis N. Update on management of diabetic foot ulcers. Ann N Y Acad Sci. 2018 Jan;1411(1):153-165. doi: 10.1111/nyas.13569. PMID: 29377202; PMCID: PMC5793889. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29377202/
